headline: Laila Gohar confects a sweet celebration in Bukhara
summary: The How To Host It columnist honours the ancient Uzbek city with sugar and samsa
body_text:
I spend a lot of time travelling and working all over the world, but a recent trip to Bukhara in Uzbekistan stopped me in my tracks. What brought me there was the inaugural Bukhara Biennial, Recipes for Broken Hearts, curated by Diana Campbell, which runs until 20 November. Historically Bukhara was an intellectual and economic centre along the Silk Roads, a place where religious and cultural traditions from all corners of the world commingled to produce a rich atmosphere of learning, craft and artistry. The Biennial brought me in dialogue with more than 200 artists and artisans from all over the world who worked collaboratively to create the more than 70 site-specific artworks and installations that are located throughout the city.

Artists such as Antony Gormley, Delcy Morelos and Wael Shawky made monumental works. For my contribution, I constructed a pavilion made of rock sugar, titled Navat Uy. Rock sugar, or navat as it is known in Uzbek, is common all over the country, but the lab
